design_philosophy.core_loop: Plan city infrastructure → zone residential/commercial/industrial → manage budget and taxes → respond to citizen needs and disasters → expand and optimize
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balance economic growth, citizen welfare, environmental sustainability, and fiscal responsibility to prevent bankruptcy and urban decay
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Architect and mayor of a thriving metropolis; creative city planner with godlike control over urban development
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Systems-first design emphasizing simulation depth and consequence over narrative; city-building as emergent storytelling through economic and social systems rather than pre-scripted events. Rejects arcade simplicity in favor of authentic municipal management complexity.

difficulty.checkpointing: Sandbox mode with persistent city state; game over triggered by extreme deficit spending and bankruptcy
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players have substantial control over challenge through budget management, tax rate adjustment per zone type, ordinance selection, and infrastructure decisions. Optional newspaper advisories and opinion polls inform strategy without forcing failure.

progression.upgrade_system: Unlock new facility types and power plant variants through city population growth and technological advancement; nine power plant varieties available (coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear, wind, hydroelectric, solar, fusion, satellite microwave)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression driven by city population milestones and simulated year advancement; facilities become available as city develops
replayability_hooks: Different city layouts and terrain elevation variations create unique planning challenges
Multiple power plant strategies with different cost/efficiency/lifespan trade-offs
Neighboring city trade dynamics encourage varied regional specialization
Disaster events (implied by newspaper warnings) create reactive gameplay moments
Tax rate and ordinance combinations enable distinct economic philosophies

world_building_techniques: Procedural newspaper generation with variable city names and humorous/relevant headlines
Environmental storytelling through urban decay visualization (darkened abandoned buildings)
Query tool reveals micro-level details (land value, traffic density, power consumption)
Special easter eggs like Neil Gaiman essay accessible via library query
Opinion polls and news alerts communicate citizen sentiment and systemic problems

faction_indicators: Residential vs. Commercial vs. Industrial zone balance reflects player economic ideology
Tax rate differentiation per zone type signals wealth distribution philosophy
Ordinance selection (implied from budget controls language) suggests policy priorities
Environmental infrastructure choices (renewable vs. nuclear power) reveal sustainability values
danger_signals: Extreme deficit spending leading to bankruptcy represents systemic economic collapse
Urban decay (abandoned buildings) signals neglected city sectors and social decline
Aging power plant infrastructure creates cascading infrastructure failure risk
Budget mismanagement can trigger cascading failures across power, water, transportation systems
environmental_storytelling: Isometric dimetric perspective reveals layered city complexity (underground pipes, subway, tunnels)
Construction graphics show active development; abandoned buildings show decay
Land value queries create economic heat map of city desirability
Traffic density visualization shows congestion and infrastructure strain
Waterfall-dependent hydroelectric dams tie power generation to natural geography

sources_used: wikipedia: SimCity 2000 - Gameplay section, Development credits, Platform/Release information
